category|Category:Tawa-jinja}} [[JA:多和神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11430645}} == Japanese content == '''Tawa Shrine''' (たわじんじゃ) is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in the Heian period. It is an {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine}}, one of the Twenty-Four Shrines of Sanuki. It is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}} {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. Under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. Located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Shido|Q11490891|WD=Q11490891|1=Shido}} and also in Maeyama, Sanuki City, Kagawa Prefecture, there are two shrines that claim to be the Engishiki-listed shrine, and debate over which one is the true Shikinaisha has continued since the late Edo period without resolution. Until the end of the Edo period, Ōtao Myōjin in Maeyama Village, Kankawa District (present-day Maeyama, Sanuki City) was worshipped as Tawa Shrine (enshrined deities: Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Taokihooi-no-Mikoto), but from around the Meiji period onward, Shido Hachimangū came to be regarded as Tawa Shrine. === Tawa Shrine (Shido, Sanuki City) === {{Shrine |Name= Tawa Shrine |Image= [[File:Tawa-jinja (Sanuki), haiden.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location= {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}{{ill|lt=Shido|Q11490891|WD=Q11490891|1=Shido}} Aza Koshimado 121 |Coordinates= {{coord|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E|display=inline}} |Enshrined deity= {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhime|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayakitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} disputed shrine
(Traditionally) {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}} Sannomiya
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded= Unknown |Main hall= |Alternate name= Shido Hachimangū |Annual festival= Second Saturday and Sunday of October |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Tawa Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E}}}} }} The date of foundation is unknown. In {{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|1=889}} ({{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} 1), it is said to have enshrined the {{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} and been renamed “Hachimangū.” It adjoins {{ill|lt=Shido-ji Temple|Q11490894|WD=Q11490894|1=Shido-ji}} and was destroyed by fire together with the temple in {{ill|lt=1479|Q6620|WD=Q6620|1=1479}} ({{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} 11). In {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610|1=1671}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 11), when Shido-ji was restored by {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Domain|Q1036915|WD=Q1036915|1=Takamatsu Domain}} lord {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Yorishige|Q3299364|WD=Q3299364|1=Matsudaira Yorishige}}, the shrine was also rebuilt. At this time it was relocated to its present site (there is also a theory that it was transferred to the current location in {{ill|lt=1623|Q6818|WD=Q6818|1=1623}} ({{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 9)). Books and artifacts collected from various regions by Matsuoka Shirabe for research are preserved within the shrine as the “{{ill|lt=Tawa Bunko|Q11430642|WD=Q11430642|1=Tawa Bunko}} (Kōbokusha)” (Important Cultural Property). ; Principal deity * {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhime|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayakitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} ; Associated deities * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119|lt=Ōsasagi-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Nintoku}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Tarashinakatsuhiko-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|lt=Okinaga-Tarashihime-no-Mikoto|1=Empress Jingū}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} === Tawa Shrine (Maeyama, Sanuki City) === {{Shrine |Name= Tawa Shrine |Image= {{Image requested|cat=Sanuki City}} |Location= {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}{{ill|lt=Maeyama|Q10898873|WD=Q10898873|1=Maeyama}} Aza Michi 2541 |Coordinates= {{coord|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E|display=inline}} |Enshrined deity= {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} disputed shrine |Founded= Unknown |Main hall= |Alternate name= Ōtao Myōjin |Annual festival= September 9 |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Tawa Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E}}}} }} The date of foundation is unknown. A shrine record states: “From Yamato Province, the Ōmiwa deity was invited during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Junna|Q329713|WD=Q329713|1=Emperor Junnin}}, in the sixth month of the first year of Tenchō (842), to Tawa-go, Kankawa District, Sanuki Province, by the founder of the Shingon sect {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|1=Kūkai}} together with Fujiwara Sa-daifu Masatomi, and thus was named Tawa Daimyōjin.” The {{ill|lt=Ōmiwa deity|Q705542|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Ōkami|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} is equated with Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto, matching the enshrined deity “{{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}.” The Ikoma Chronicle states, “This shrine (in Maeyama) is the Tawa Shrine of Kankawa District, Sanuki Province, listed in the Engishiki.” The Zensan-shi records: “Tawa Shrine is located in Ōtawa, Maeyama Village. The Gokuraku-ji record says: In the eighth year of Engi (908), in the fourth month of summer, Tawa Shrine and Minono-hiko Shrine were established; this likely refers to their being listed among the government shrines. Now only foundation stones remain, and within there is a small shrine.”{{Cite web |title=Zensan-shi {{!}} Kansai University Digital Archive |url=https://www.iiif.ku-orcas.kansai-u.ac.jp/hakuen_bunko/002500426#?page=1 |website=www.iiif.ku-orcas.kansai-u.ac.jp |access-date=2024-12-19}} ; Principal deity * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} ; Associated deities * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taokihooi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} === Controversy concerning the Shikinaisha === In the late Edo period, {{ill|lt=Matsuoka Shirabe|Q6537602|WD=Q6537602|1=Matsuoka Mitsugi}}, the chief priest of Shido Hachimangū (an artist of the “Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province,” later serving as priest of Kotohira-gū), was also known as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kokugaku scholar|1=UNKNOWN}} and contributed greatly to the Takamatsu Domain. He suddenly asserted that “Shido Hachimangū is the Tawa Shrine (Sanuki Sannomiya) listed in the Engishiki.” In his work *Sanuki Province Official Shrines Examination*{{Cite web |title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/815400 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} (Meiji 12), Matsuoka wrote: “The Tawa Shrine in Maeyama enshrines [https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2634.html Koyosotahobime], {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taokihooi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, or else {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and is called Ōtao after their names, and therefore is not Tawa Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=※1|1=UNKNOWN}}. Just as there is a Kōzaki Shrine in Kōzaki-go, Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go. In a document called the ‘Onharai-fuchō’ {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=※2|1=UNKNOWN}}, which my Matsuoka family has inherited for generations from the Okada Daifu family, priests of the Outer Shrine, there is an entry for Shido-ura that reads ‘Tawa no kannushi-dono,’ which can be said to be proof that the shrine I serve is Tawa Shrine. Though worm-eaten, I reproduce a copy below. As shown by the Onharai-fuchō, Tama-no-ura (Shido Bay) is Tawa-no-ura, and the land of Shido is Tawa-go.” From the Meiji period onward, Shido Hachimangū thus came to be widely known as the Engishiki-listed Tawa Shrine. ※1 {{ill|lt=However, upon checking the Kagawa Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer, neither Koyosotahobime nor Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto are enshrined at the Maeyama Tawa Shrine.|UNKNOWN|lt=According to the Kagawa Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer, neither Koyosotahobime nor Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto are enshrined at the Maeyama Tawa Shrine.}} There are differing theories regarding the place name Ōtawa in Maeyama Village: one holds that “tawa” derives from “tawamu” (to bend), meaning a bent mountain, that is, a mountain pass; another suggests it comes from “tao” (tail of a rice field), meaning the end of the paddies extending from the plain (at the mountain’s edge).{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} ※2 Okada Daifu copy: “Sanuki Dōja Ippen Nikki,” Ansei 3 (1856), a record of visits made in Eiroku 8 (1565) by Ise shrine priests (Ise Onshi), messengers of Ise Grand Shrine, who toured their Sanuki parishioners to receive first-fruits offerings. It records their travel routes, regions, parishioner names, and the contents and quantities of Ise souvenirs distributed (held by Kanzakura Shrine, Kōnan Town).{{Cite web |title=Exhibited materials: “A City Open to the Sea—Takamatsu: 900 Years as a Port City—Special Exhibition” {{!}} NDL Search {{!}} National Diet Library |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I000009429918 |website=National Diet Library Search (NDL Search) |access-date=2025-01-22 |language=ja}} Meanwhile, as local historical research progressed, a theory emerged that Tawa-go was not Shido or Maeyama but Tsuruba in Tsuda Town. According to the *Newly Compiled History of Kagawa Prefecture*{{Cite web |title=Newly Compiled History of Kagawa Prefecture {{!}} NDL Search {{!}} National Diet Library |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I000000941988 |website=National Diet Library Search (NDL Search) |access-date=2024-12-20 |language=ja}} (published 1953), a “Daijōkan decree” held by {{ill|lt=Anrakuju-in|Q2852420|WD=Q2852420|lt=Anrakujū-ji|1=Anrakuju-in}} in Kyoto (dated August 19, {{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459|1=Kōji (Heian period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1143|Q19617|WD=Q19617|lt=1143|1=1143}})) describes the boundaries of Tomita Estate, stating: “To the east, the boundary is the border of Ōuchi District; to the west, from within Ishida-go at the northeastern corner, the Funaki River (present-day Sendan River) and the southern great road of Ishizaki (Nankaidō), along the bank of Minami-izumi; to the south, the border with Awa Province; to the north, the mountain peaks forming the boundary between Tawa and Kanzaki.” Thus it is clear that to the north of Tomita Estate lay the two districts of Kanzaki and Tawa. This also accords with the old maps and travel routes (regions) of the Ise priests found in the copy of the “Onharai-fuchō” (= “Sanuki Dōja Ippen Nikki”) cited by Matsuoka Shirabe in *Sanuki Province Official Shrines Examination*. Therefore, if one accepts Matsuoka Shirabe’s premise that “Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go,” then the Tawa Shrine in Shido would also be in error. If one follows Matsuoka Shirabe’s assertion that “the shrine located in Tawa-go is Tawa Shrine,” there is also a theory that Ōmori Shrine, enshrined within the grounds of a Tenmangū located in the mountains of the Tsuruba area, should be identified as Tawa Shrine. However, due to a lack of sources, the whereabouts of the Sanuki Sannomiya “Tawa Shrine” remain unknown. There is no doubt that, throughout history, both the Tawa Shrine in Shido and the Tawa Shrine in Maeyama have been regarded and worshipped as the Engishiki-listed Tawa Shrine.{{Cite web |title=History of Shido Town (New Edition), Vols. 1–2 – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2037 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}}{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} If the premise that “Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go” collapses, or if definitive sources are discovered, history may yet be rewritten.{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} === References === * *Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province*{{Cite web |title=Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province category|Category:Tawa-jinja}} [[JA:多和神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11430645}} == Japanese content == '''Tawa Shrine''' (たわじんじゃ) is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in the Heian period. It is an {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine}}, one of the Twenty-Four Shrines of Sanuki. It is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}} {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. Under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. Located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Shido|Q11490891|WD=Q11490891|1=Shido}} and also in Maeyama, Sanuki City, Kagawa Prefecture, there are two shrines that claim to be the Engishiki-listed shrine, and debate over which one is the true Shikinaisha has continued since the late Edo period without resolution. Until the end of the Edo period, Ōtao Myōjin in Maeyama Village, Kankawa District (present-day Maeyama, Sanuki City) was worshipped as Tawa Shrine (enshrined deities: Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Taokihooi-no-Mikoto), but from around the Meiji period onward, Shido Hachimangū came to be regarded as Tawa Shrine. === Tawa Shrine (Shido, Sanuki City) === {{Shrine |Name= Tawa Shrine |Image= [[File:Tawa-jinja (Sanuki), haiden.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location= {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}{{ill|lt=Shido|Q11490891|WD=Q11490891|1=Shido}} Aza Koshimado 121 |Coordinates= {{coord|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E|display=inline}} |Enshrined deity= {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhime|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayakitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} disputed shrine
(Traditionally) {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}} Sannomiya
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded= Unknown |Main hall= |Alternate name= Shido Hachimangū |Annual festival= Second Saturday and Sunday of October |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Tawa Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E}}}} }} The date of foundation is unknown. In {{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|1=889}} ({{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} 1), it is said to have enshrined the {{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} and been renamed “Hachimangū.” It adjoins {{ill|lt=Shido-ji Temple|Q11490894|WD=Q11490894|1=Shido-ji}} and was destroyed by fire together with the temple in {{ill|lt=1479|Q6620|WD=Q6620|1=1479}} ({{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} 11). In {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610|1=1671}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 11), when Shido-ji was restored by {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Domain|Q1036915|WD=Q1036915|1=Takamatsu Domain}} lord {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Yorishige|Q3299364|WD=Q3299364|1=Matsudaira Yorishige}}, the shrine was also rebuilt. At this time it was relocated to its present site (there is also a theory that it was transferred to the current location in {{ill|lt=1623|Q6818|WD=Q6818|1=1623}} ({{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 9)). Books and artifacts collected from various regions by Matsuoka Shirabe for research are preserved within the shrine as the “{{ill|lt=Tawa Bunko|Q11430642|WD=Q11430642|1=Tawa Bunko}} (Kōbokusha)” (Important Cultural Property). ; Principal deity * {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhime|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayakitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} ; Associated deities * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119|lt=Ōsasagi-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Nintoku}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Tarashinakatsuhiko-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|lt=Okinaga-Tarashihime-no-Mikoto|1=Empress Jingū}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} === Tawa Shrine (Maeyama, Sanuki City) === {{Shrine |Name= Tawa Shrine |Image= {{Image requested|cat=Sanuki City}} |Location= {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}{{ill|lt=Maeyama|Q10898873|WD=Q10898873|1=Maeyama}} Aza Michi 2541 |Coordinates= {{coord|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E|display=inline}} |Enshrined deity= {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} disputed shrine |Founded= Unknown |Main hall= |Alternate name= Ōtao Myōjin |Annual festival= September 9 |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Tawa Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E}}}} }} The date of foundation is unknown. A shrine record states: “From Yamato Province, the Ōmiwa deity was invited during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Junna|Q329713|WD=Q329713|1=Emperor Junnin}}, in the sixth month of the first year of Tenchō (842), to Tawa-go, Kankawa District, Sanuki Province, by the founder of the Shingon sect {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|1=Kūkai}} together with Fujiwara Sa-daifu Masatomi, and thus was named Tawa Daimyōjin.” The {{ill|lt=Ōmiwa deity|Q705542|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Ōkami|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} is equated with Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto, matching the enshrined deity “{{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}.” The Ikoma Chronicle states, “This shrine (in Maeyama) is the Tawa Shrine of Kankawa District, Sanuki Province, listed in the Engishiki.” The Zensan-shi records: “Tawa Shrine is located in Ōtawa, Maeyama Village. The Gokuraku-ji record says: In the eighth year of Engi (908), in the fourth month of summer, Tawa Shrine and Minono-hiko Shrine were established; this likely refers to their being listed among the government shrines. Now only foundation stones remain, and within there is a small shrine.”{{Cite web |title=Zensan-shi {{!}} Kansai University Digital Archive |url=https://www.iiif.ku-orcas.kansai-u.ac.jp/hakuen_bunko/002500426#?page=1 |website=www.iiif.ku-orcas.kansai-u.ac.jp |access-date=2024-12-19}} ; Principal deity * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} ; Associated deities * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taokihooi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} === Controversy concerning the Shikinaisha === In the late Edo period, {{ill|lt=Matsuoka Shirabe|Q6537602|WD=Q6537602|1=Matsuoka Mitsugi}}, the chief priest of Shido Hachimangū (an artist of the “Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province,” later serving as priest of Kotohira-gū), was also known as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kokugaku scholar|1=UNKNOWN}} and contributed greatly to the Takamatsu Domain. He suddenly asserted that “Shido Hachimangū is the Tawa Shrine (Sanuki Sannomiya) listed in the Engishiki.” In his work *Sanuki Province Official Shrines Examination*{{Cite web |title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/815400 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} (Meiji 12), Matsuoka wrote: “The Tawa Shrine in Maeyama enshrines [https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2634.html Koyosotahobime], {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taokihooi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, or else {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and is called Ōtao after their names, and therefore is not Tawa Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=※1|1=UNKNOWN}}. Just as there is a Kōzaki Shrine in Kōzaki-go, Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go. In a document called the ‘Onharai-fuchō’ {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=※2|1=UNKNOWN}}, which my Matsuoka family has inherited for generations from the Okada Daifu family, priests of the Outer Shrine, there is an entry for Shido-ura that reads ‘Tawa no kannushi-dono,’ which can be said to be proof that the shrine I serve is Tawa Shrine. Though worm-eaten, I reproduce a copy below. As shown by the Onharai-fuchō, Tama-no-ura (Shido Bay) is Tawa-no-ura, and the land of Shido is Tawa-go.” From the Meiji period onward, Shido Hachimangū thus came to be widely known as the Engishiki-listed Tawa Shrine. ※1 {{ill|lt=However, upon checking the Kagawa Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer, neither Koyosotahobime nor Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto are enshrined at the Maeyama Tawa Shrine.|UNKNOWN|lt=According to the Kagawa Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer, neither Koyosotahobime nor Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto are enshrined at the Maeyama Tawa Shrine.}} There are differing theories regarding the place name Ōtawa in Maeyama Village: one holds that “tawa” derives from “tawamu” (to bend), meaning a bent mountain, that is, a mountain pass; another suggests it comes from “tao” (tail of a rice field), meaning the end of the paddies extending from the plain (at the mountain’s edge).{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} ※2 Okada Daifu copy: “Sanuki Dōja Ippen Nikki,” Ansei 3 (1856), a record of visits made in Eiroku 8 (1565) by Ise shrine priests (Ise Onshi), messengers of Ise Grand Shrine, who toured their Sanuki parishioners to receive first-fruits offerings. It records their travel routes, regions, parishioner names, and the contents and quantities of Ise souvenirs distributed (held by Kanzakura Shrine, Kōnan Town).{{Cite web |title=Exhibited materials: “A City Open to the Sea—Takamatsu: 900 Years as a Port City—Special Exhibition” {{!}} NDL Search {{!}} National Diet Library |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I000009429918 |website=National Diet Library Search (NDL Search) |access-date=2025-01-22 |language=ja}} Meanwhile, as local historical research progressed, a theory emerged that Tawa-go was not Shido or Maeyama but Tsuruba in Tsuda Town. According to the *Newly Compiled History of Kagawa Prefecture*{{Cite web |title=Newly Compiled History of Kagawa Prefecture {{!}} NDL Search {{!}} National Diet Library |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I000000941988 |website=National Diet Library Search (NDL Search) |access-date=2024-12-20 |language=ja}} (published 1953), a “Daijōkan decree” held by {{ill|lt=Anrakuju-in|Q2852420|WD=Q2852420|lt=Anrakujū-ji|1=Anrakuju-in}} in Kyoto (dated August 19, {{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459|1=Kōji (Heian period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1143|Q19617|WD=Q19617|lt=1143|1=1143}})) describes the boundaries of Tomita Estate, stating: “To the east, the boundary is the border of Ōuchi District; to the west, from within Ishida-go at the northeastern corner, the Funaki River (present-day Sendan River) and the southern great road of Ishizaki (Nankaidō), along the bank of Minami-izumi; to the south, the border with Awa Province; to the north, the mountain peaks forming the boundary between Tawa and Kanzaki.” Thus it is clear that to the north of Tomita Estate lay the two districts of Kanzaki and Tawa. This also accords with the old maps and travel routes (regions) of the Ise priests found in the copy of the “Onharai-fuchō” (= “Sanuki Dōja Ippen Nikki”) cited by Matsuoka Shirabe in *Sanuki Province Official Shrines Examination*. Therefore, if one accepts Matsuoka Shirabe’s premise that “Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go,” then the Tawa Shrine in Shido would also be in error. If one follows Matsuoka Shirabe’s assertion that “the shrine located in Tawa-go is Tawa Shrine,” there is also a theory that Ōmori Shrine, enshrined within the grounds of a Tenmangū located in the mountains of the Tsuruba area, should be identified as Tawa Shrine. However, due to a lack of sources, the whereabouts of the Sanuki Sannomiya “Tawa Shrine” remain unknown. There is no doubt that, throughout history, both the Tawa Shrine in Shido and the Tawa Shrine in Maeyama have been regarded and worshipped as the Engishiki-listed Tawa Shrine.{{Cite web |title=History of Shido Town (New Edition), Vols. 1–2 – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2037 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}}{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} If the premise that “Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go” collapses, or if definitive sources are discovered, history may yet be rewritten.{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} === References === * *Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province*{{Cite web |title=Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province category|Category:Tawa-jinja}} [[JA:多和神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11430645}} == Japanese content == '''Tawa Shrine''' (たわじんじゃ) is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in the Heian period. It is an {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine}}, one of the Twenty-Four Shrines of Sanuki. It is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}} {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. Under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. Located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Shido|Q11490891|WD=Q11490891|1=Shido}} and also in Maeyama, Sanuki City, Kagawa Prefecture, there are two shrines that claim to be the Engishiki-listed shrine, and debate over which one is the true Shikinaisha has continued since the late Edo period without resolution. Until the end of the Edo period, Ōtao Myōjin in Maeyama Village, Kankawa District (present-day Maeyama, Sanuki City) was worshipped as Tawa Shrine (enshrined deities: Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Taokihooi-no-Mikoto), but from around the Meiji period onward, Shido Hachimangū came to be regarded as Tawa Shrine. === Tawa Shrine (Shido, Sanuki City) === {{Shrine |Name= Tawa Shrine |Image= [[File:Tawa-jinja (Sanuki), haiden.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location= {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}{{ill|lt=Shido|Q11490891|WD=Q11490891|1=Shido}} Aza Koshimado 121 |Coordinates= {{coord|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E|display=inline}} |Enshrined deity= {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhime|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayakitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} disputed shrine
(Traditionally) {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}} Sannomiya
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded= Unknown |Main hall= |Alternate name= Shido Hachimangū |Annual festival= Second Saturday and Sunday of October |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Tawa Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E}}}} }} The date of foundation is unknown. In {{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|1=889}} ({{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} 1), it is said to have enshrined the {{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} and been renamed “Hachimangū.” It adjoins {{ill|lt=Shido-ji Temple|Q11490894|WD=Q11490894|1=Shido-ji}} and was destroyed by fire together with the temple in {{ill|lt=1479|Q6620|WD=Q6620|1=1479}} ({{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} 11). In {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610|1=1671}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 11), when Shido-ji was restored by {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Domain|Q1036915|WD=Q1036915|1=Takamatsu Domain}} lord {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Yorishige|Q3299364|WD=Q3299364|1=Matsudaira Yorishige}}, the shrine was also rebuilt. At this time it was relocated to its present site (there is also a theory that it was transferred to the current location in {{ill|lt=1623|Q6818|WD=Q6818|1=1623}} ({{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 9)). Books and artifacts collected from various regions by Matsuoka Shirabe for research are preserved within the shrine as the “{{ill|lt=Tawa Bunko|Q11430642|WD=Q11430642|1=Tawa Bunko}} (Kōbokusha)” (Important Cultural Property). ; Principal deity * {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhime|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayakitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} ; Associated deities * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119|lt=Ōsasagi-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Nintoku}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Tarashinakatsuhiko-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|lt=Okinaga-Tarashihime-no-Mikoto|1=Empress Jingū}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} === Tawa Shrine (Maeyama, Sanuki City) === {{Shrine |Name= Tawa Shrine |Image= {{Image requested|cat=Sanuki City}} |Location= {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}{{ill|lt=Maeyama|Q10898873|WD=Q10898873|1=Maeyama}} Aza Michi 2541 |Coordinates= {{coord|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E|display=inline}} |Enshrined deity= {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} disputed shrine |Founded= Unknown |Main hall= |Alternate name= Ōtao Myōjin |Annual festival= September 9 |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Tawa Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E}}}} }} The date of foundation is unknown. A shrine record states: “From Yamato Province, the Ōmiwa deity was invited during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Junna|Q329713|WD=Q329713|1=Emperor Junnin}}, in the sixth month of the first year of Tenchō (842), to Tawa-go, Kankawa District, Sanuki Province, by the founder of the Shingon sect {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|1=Kūkai}} together with Fujiwara Sa-daifu Masatomi, and thus was named Tawa Daimyōjin.” The {{ill|lt=Ōmiwa deity|Q705542|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Ōkami|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} is equated with Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto, matching the enshrined deity “{{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}.” The Ikoma Chronicle states, “This shrine (in Maeyama) is the Tawa Shrine of Kankawa District, Sanuki Province, listed in the Engishiki.” The Zensan-shi records: “Tawa Shrine is located in Ōtawa, Maeyama Village. The Gokuraku-ji record says: In the eighth year of Engi (908), in the fourth month of summer, Tawa Shrine and Minono-hiko Shrine were established; this likely refers to their being listed among the government shrines. Now only foundation stones remain, and within there is a small shrine.”{{Cite web |title=Zensan-shi {{!}} Kansai University Digital Archive |url=https://www.iiif.ku-orcas.kansai-u.ac.jp/hakuen_bunko/002500426#?page=1 |website=www.iiif.ku-orcas.kansai-u.ac.jp |access-date=2024-12-19}} ; Principal deity * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} ; Associated deities * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taokihooi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} === Controversy concerning the Shikinaisha === In the late Edo period, {{ill|lt=Matsuoka Shirabe|Q6537602|WD=Q6537602|1=Matsuoka Mitsugi}}, the chief priest of Shido Hachimangū (an artist of the “Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province,” later serving as priest of Kotohira-gū), was also known as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kokugaku scholar|1=UNKNOWN}} and contributed greatly to the Takamatsu Domain. He suddenly asserted that “Shido Hachimangū is the Tawa Shrine (Sanuki Sannomiya) listed in the Engishiki.” In his work *Sanuki Province Official Shrines Examination*{{Cite web |title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/815400 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} (Meiji 12), Matsuoka wrote: “The Tawa Shrine in Maeyama enshrines [https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2634.html Koyosotahobime], {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taokihooi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, or else {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and is called Ōtao after their names, and therefore is not Tawa Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=※1|1=UNKNOWN}}. Just as there is a Kōzaki Shrine in Kōzaki-go, Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go. In a document called the ‘Onharai-fuchō’ {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=※2|1=UNKNOWN}}, which my Matsuoka family has inherited for generations from the Okada Daifu family, priests of the Outer Shrine, there is an entry for Shido-ura that reads ‘Tawa no kannushi-dono,’ which can be said to be proof that the shrine I serve is Tawa Shrine. Though worm-eaten, I reproduce a copy below. As shown by the Onharai-fuchō, Tama-no-ura (Shido Bay) is Tawa-no-ura, and the land of Shido is Tawa-go.” From the Meiji period onward, Shido Hachimangū thus came to be widely known as the Engishiki-listed Tawa Shrine. ※1 {{ill|lt=However, upon checking the Kagawa Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer, neither Koyosotahobime nor Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto are enshrined at the Maeyama Tawa Shrine.|UNKNOWN|lt=According to the Kagawa Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer, neither Koyosotahobime nor Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto are enshrined at the Maeyama Tawa Shrine.}} There are differing theories regarding the place name Ōtawa in Maeyama Village: one holds that “tawa” derives from “tawamu” (to bend), meaning a bent mountain, that is, a mountain pass; another suggests it comes from “tao” (tail of a rice field), meaning the end of the paddies extending from the plain (at the mountain’s edge).{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} ※2 Okada Daifu copy: “Sanuki Dōja Ippen Nikki,” Ansei 3 (1856), a record of visits made in Eiroku 8 (1565) by Ise shrine priests (Ise Onshi), messengers of Ise Grand Shrine, who toured their Sanuki parishioners to receive first-fruits offerings. It records their travel routes, regions, parishioner names, and the contents and quantities of Ise souvenirs distributed (held by Kanzakura Shrine, Kōnan Town).{{Cite web |title=Exhibited materials: “A City Open to the Sea—Takamatsu: 900 Years as a Port City—Special Exhibition” {{!}} NDL Search {{!}} National Diet Library |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I000009429918 |website=National Diet Library Search (NDL Search) |access-date=2025-01-22 |language=ja}} Meanwhile, as local historical research progressed, a theory emerged that Tawa-go was not Shido or Maeyama but Tsuruba in Tsuda Town. According to the *Newly Compiled History of Kagawa Prefecture*{{Cite web |title=Newly Compiled History of Kagawa Prefecture {{!}} NDL Search {{!}} National Diet Library |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I000000941988 |website=National Diet Library Search (NDL Search) |access-date=2024-12-20 |language=ja}} (published 1953), a “Daijōkan decree” held by {{ill|lt=Anrakuju-in|Q2852420|WD=Q2852420|lt=Anrakujū-ji|1=Anrakuju-in}} in Kyoto (dated August 19, {{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459|1=Kōji (Heian period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1143|Q19617|WD=Q19617|lt=1143|1=1143}})) describes the boundaries of Tomita Estate, stating: “To the east, the boundary is the border of Ōuchi District; to the west, from within Ishida-go at the northeastern corner, the Funaki River (present-day Sendan River) and the southern great road of Ishizaki (Nankaidō), along the bank of Minami-izumi; to the south, the border with Awa Province; to the north, the mountain peaks forming the boundary between Tawa and Kanzaki.” Thus it is clear that to the north of Tomita Estate lay the two districts of Kanzaki and Tawa. This also accords with the old maps and travel routes (regions) of the Ise priests found in the copy of the “Onharai-fuchō” (= “Sanuki Dōja Ippen Nikki”) cited by Matsuoka Shirabe in *Sanuki Province Official Shrines Examination*. Therefore, if one accepts Matsuoka Shirabe’s premise that “Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go,” then the Tawa Shrine in Shido would also be in error. If one follows Matsuoka Shirabe’s assertion that “the shrine located in Tawa-go is Tawa Shrine,” there is also a theory that Ōmori Shrine, enshrined within the grounds of a Tenmangū located in the mountains of the Tsuruba area, should be identified as Tawa Shrine. However, due to a lack of sources, the whereabouts of the Sanuki Sannomiya “Tawa Shrine” remain unknown. There is no doubt that, throughout history, both the Tawa Shrine in Shido and the Tawa Shrine in Maeyama have been regarded and worshipped as the Engishiki-listed Tawa Shrine.{{Cite web |title=History of Shido Town (New Edition), Vols. 1–2 – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2037 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}}{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} If the premise that “Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go” collapses, or if definitive sources are discovered, history may yet be rewritten.{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} === References === * *Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province*{{Cite web |title=Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province category|Category:Tawa-jinja}} [[JA:多和神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11430645}} == Japanese content == '''Tawa Shrine''' (たわじんじゃ) is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in the Heian period. It is an {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine}}, one of the Twenty-Four Shrines of Sanuki. It is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}} {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. Under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. Located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Shido|Q11490891|WD=Q11490891|1=Shido}} and also in Maeyama, Sanuki City, Kagawa Prefecture, there are two shrines that claim to be the Engishiki-listed shrine, and debate over which one is the true Shikinaisha has continued since the late Edo period without resolution. Until the end of the Edo period, Ōtao Myōjin in Maeyama Village, Kankawa District (present-day Maeyama, Sanuki City) was worshipped as Tawa Shrine (enshrined deities: Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Taokihooi-no-Mikoto), but from around the Meiji period onward, Shido Hachimangū came to be regarded as Tawa Shrine. === Tawa Shrine (Shido, Sanuki City) === {{Shrine |Name= Tawa Shrine |Image= [[File:Tawa-jinja (Sanuki), haiden.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location= {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}{{ill|lt=Shido|Q11490891|WD=Q11490891|1=Shido}} Aza Koshimado 121 |Coordinates= {{coord|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E|display=inline}} |Enshrined deity= {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhime|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayakitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} disputed shrine
(Traditionally) {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}} Sannomiya
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded= Unknown |Main hall= |Alternate name= Shido Hachimangū |Annual festival= Second Saturday and Sunday of October |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Tawa Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E}}}} }} The date of foundation is unknown. In {{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|1=889}} ({{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} 1), it is said to have enshrined the {{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} and been renamed “Hachimangū.” It adjoins {{ill|lt=Shido-ji Temple|Q11490894|WD=Q11490894|1=Shido-ji}} and was destroyed by fire together with the temple in {{ill|lt=1479|Q6620|WD=Q6620|1=1479}} ({{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} 11). In {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610|1=1671}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 11), when Shido-ji was restored by {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Domain|Q1036915|WD=Q1036915|1=Takamatsu Domain}} lord {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Yorishige|Q3299364|WD=Q3299364|1=Matsudaira Yorishige}}, the shrine was also rebuilt. At this time it was relocated to its present site (there is also a theory that it was transferred to the current location in {{ill|lt=1623|Q6818|WD=Q6818|1=1623}} ({{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 9)). Books and artifacts collected from various regions by Matsuoka Shirabe for research are preserved within the shrine as the “{{ill|lt=Tawa Bunko|Q11430642|WD=Q11430642|1=Tawa Bunko}} (Kōbokusha)” (Important Cultural Property). ; Principal deity * {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhime|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayakitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} ; Associated deities * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119|lt=Ōsasagi-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Nintoku}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Tarashinakatsuhiko-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|lt=Okinaga-Tarashihime-no-Mikoto|1=Empress Jingū}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} === Tawa Shrine (Maeyama, Sanuki City) === {{Shrine |Name= Tawa Shrine |Image= {{Image requested|cat=Sanuki City}} |Location= {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}{{ill|lt=Maeyama|Q10898873|WD=Q10898873|1=Maeyama}} Aza Michi 2541 |Coordinates= {{coord|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E|display=inline}} |Enshrined deity= {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} disputed shrine |Founded= Unknown |Main hall= |Alternate name= Ōtao Myōjin |Annual festival= September 9 |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Tawa Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E}}}} }} The date of foundation is unknown. A shrine record states: “From Yamato Province, the Ōmiwa deity was invited during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Junna|Q329713|WD=Q329713|1=Emperor Junnin}}, in the sixth month of the first year of Tenchō (842), to Tawa-go, Kankawa District, Sanuki Province, by the founder of the Shingon sect {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|1=Kūkai}} together with Fujiwara Sa-daifu Masatomi, and thus was named Tawa Daimyōjin.” The {{ill|lt=Ōmiwa deity|Q705542|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Ōkami|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} is equated with Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto, matching the enshrined deity “{{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}.” The Ikoma Chronicle states, “This shrine (in Maeyama) is the Tawa Shrine of Kankawa District, Sanuki Province, listed in the Engishiki.” The Zensan-shi records: “Tawa Shrine is located in Ōtawa, Maeyama Village. The Gokuraku-ji record says: In the eighth year of Engi (908), in the fourth month of summer, Tawa Shrine and Minono-hiko Shrine were established; this likely refers to their being listed among the government shrines. Now only foundation stones remain, and within there is a small shrine.”{{Cite web |title=Zensan-shi {{!}} Kansai University Digital Archive |url=https://www.iiif.ku-orcas.kansai-u.ac.jp/hakuen_bunko/002500426#?page=1 |website=www.iiif.ku-orcas.kansai-u.ac.jp |access-date=2024-12-19}} ; Principal deity * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} ; Associated deities * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taokihooi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} === Controversy concerning the Shikinaisha === In the late Edo period, {{ill|lt=Matsuoka Shirabe|Q6537602|WD=Q6537602|1=Matsuoka Mitsugi}}, the chief priest of Shido Hachimangū (an artist of the “Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province,” later serving as priest of Kotohira-gū), was also known as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kokugaku scholar|1=UNKNOWN}} and contributed greatly to the Takamatsu Domain. He suddenly asserted that “Shido Hachimangū is the Tawa Shrine (Sanuki Sannomiya) listed in the Engishiki.” In his work *Sanuki Province Official Shrines Examination*{{Cite web |title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/815400 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} (Meiji 12), Matsuoka wrote: “The Tawa Shrine in Maeyama enshrines [https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2634.html Koyosotahobime], {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taokihooi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, or else {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and is called Ōtao after their names, and therefore is not Tawa Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=※1|1=UNKNOWN}}. Just as there is a Kōzaki Shrine in Kōzaki-go, Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go. In a document called the ‘Onharai-fuchō’ {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=※2|1=UNKNOWN}}, which my Matsuoka family has inherited for generations from the Okada Daifu family, priests of the Outer Shrine, there is an entry for Shido-ura that reads ‘Tawa no kannushi-dono,’ which can be said to be proof that the shrine I serve is Tawa Shrine. Though worm-eaten, I reproduce a copy below. As shown by the Onharai-fuchō, Tama-no-ura (Shido Bay) is Tawa-no-ura, and the land of Shido is Tawa-go.” From the Meiji period onward, Shido Hachimangū thus came to be widely known as the Engishiki-listed Tawa Shrine. ※1 {{ill|lt=However, upon checking the Kagawa Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer, neither Koyosotahobime nor Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto are enshrined at the Maeyama Tawa Shrine.|UNKNOWN|lt=According to the Kagawa Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer, neither Koyosotahobime nor Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto are enshrined at the Maeyama Tawa Shrine.}} There are differing theories regarding the place name Ōtawa in Maeyama Village: one holds that “tawa” derives from “tawamu” (to bend), meaning a bent mountain, that is, a mountain pass; another suggests it comes from “tao” (tail of a rice field), meaning the end of the paddies extending from the plain (at the mountain’s edge).{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} ※2 Okada Daifu copy: “Sanuki Dōja Ippen Nikki,” Ansei 3 (1856), a record of visits made in Eiroku 8 (1565) by Ise shrine priests (Ise Onshi), messengers of Ise Grand Shrine, who toured their Sanuki parishioners to receive first-fruits offerings. It records their travel routes, regions, parishioner names, and the contents and quantities of Ise souvenirs distributed (held by Kanzakura Shrine, Kōnan Town).{{Cite web |title=Exhibited materials: “A City Open to the Sea—Takamatsu: 900 Years as a Port City—Special Exhibition” {{!}} NDL Search {{!}} National Diet Library |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I000009429918 |website=National Diet Library Search (NDL Search) |access-date=2025-01-22 |language=ja}} Meanwhile, as local historical research progressed, a theory emerged that Tawa-go was not Shido or Maeyama but Tsuruba in Tsuda Town. According to the *Newly Compiled History of Kagawa Prefecture*{{Cite web |title=Newly Compiled History of Kagawa Prefecture {{!}} NDL Search {{!}} National Diet Library |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I000000941988 |website=National Diet Library Search (NDL Search) |access-date=2024-12-20 |language=ja}} (published 1953), a “Daijōkan decree” held by {{ill|lt=Anrakuju-in|Q2852420|WD=Q2852420|lt=Anrakujū-ji|1=Anrakuju-in}} in Kyoto (dated August 19, {{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459|1=Kōji (Heian period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1143|Q19617|WD=Q19617|lt=1143|1=1143}})) describes the boundaries of Tomita Estate, stating: “To the east, the boundary is the border of Ōuchi District; to the west, from within Ishida-go at the northeastern corner, the Funaki River (present-day Sendan River) and the southern great road of Ishizaki (Nankaidō), along the bank of Minami-izumi; to the south, the border with Awa Province; to the north, the mountain peaks forming the boundary between Tawa and Kanzaki.” Thus it is clear that to the north of Tomita Estate lay the two districts of Kanzaki and Tawa. This also accords with the old maps and travel routes (regions) of the Ise priests found in the copy of the “Onharai-fuchō” (= “Sanuki Dōja Ippen Nikki”) cited by Matsuoka Shirabe in *Sanuki Province Official Shrines Examination*. Therefore, if one accepts Matsuoka Shirabe’s premise that “Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go,” then the Tawa Shrine in Shido would also be in error. If one follows Matsuoka Shirabe’s assertion that “the shrine located in Tawa-go is Tawa Shrine,” there is also a theory that Ōmori Shrine, enshrined within the grounds of a Tenmangū located in the mountains of the Tsuruba area, should be identified as Tawa Shrine. However, due to a lack of sources, the whereabouts of the Sanuki Sannomiya “Tawa Shrine” remain unknown. There is no doubt that, throughout history, both the Tawa Shrine in Shido and the Tawa Shrine in Maeyama have been regarded and worshipped as the Engishiki-listed Tawa Shrine.{{Cite web |title=History of Shido Town (New Edition), Vols. 1–2 – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2037 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}}{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} If the premise that “Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go” collapses, or if definitive sources are discovered, history may yet be rewritten.{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} === References === * *Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province*{{Cite web |title=Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province Shrine database ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '香川県さぬき市志度字越窓121', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Tawa-jinja}} [[JA:多和神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11430645}} == Japanese content == '''Tawa Shrine''' (たわじんじゃ) is listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in the Heian period. It is an {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki shrine}}, one of the Twenty-Four Shrines of Sanuki. It is traditionally said to be the {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}} {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}. Under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. Located in {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Shido|Q11490891|WD=Q11490891|1=Shido}} and also in Maeyama, Sanuki City, Kagawa Prefecture, there are two shrines that claim to be the Engishiki-listed shrine, and debate over which one is the true Shikinaisha has continued since the late Edo period without resolution. Until the end of the Edo period, Ōtao Myōjin in Maeyama Village, Kankawa District (present-day Maeyama, Sanuki City) was worshipped as Tawa Shrine (enshrined deities: Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto and Taokihooi-no-Mikoto), but from around the Meiji period onward, Shido Hachimangū came to be regarded as Tawa Shrine. === Tawa Shrine (Shido, Sanuki City) === {{Shrine |Name= Tawa Shrine |Image= [[File:Tawa-jinja (Sanuki), haiden.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location= {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}{{ill|lt=Shido|Q11490891|WD=Q11490891|1=Shido}} Aza Koshimado 121 |Coordinates= {{coord|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E|display=inline}} |Enshrined deity= {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhime|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayakitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} disputed shrine
(Traditionally) {{ill|lt=Sanuki Province|Q929682|WD=Q929682|1=Sanuki Province}} Sannomiya
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded= Unknown |Main hall= |Alternate name= Shido Hachimangū |Annual festival= Second Saturday and Sunday of October |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Tawa Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|19|18.1|N|134|9|54.4|E}}}} }} The date of foundation is unknown. In {{ill|lt=889|Q23833|WD=Q23833|1=889}} ({{ill|lt=Kanpyō|Q1346292|WD=Q1346292|1=Kanpyō (era)}} 1), it is said to have enshrined the {{ill|lt=Hachiman deity|Q261637|WD=Q261637|1=Hachiman}} and been renamed “Hachimangū.” It adjoins {{ill|lt=Shido-ji Temple|Q11490894|WD=Q11490894|1=Shido-ji}} and was destroyed by fire together with the temple in {{ill|lt=1479|Q6620|WD=Q6620|1=1479}} ({{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei|1=Bunmei}} 11). In {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610|1=1671}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 11), when Shido-ji was restored by {{ill|lt=Takamatsu Domain|Q1036915|WD=Q1036915|1=Takamatsu Domain}} lord {{ill|lt=Matsudaira Yorishige|Q3299364|WD=Q3299364|1=Matsudaira Yorishige}}, the shrine was also rebuilt. At this time it was relocated to its present site (there is also a theory that it was transferred to the current location in {{ill|lt=1623|Q6818|WD=Q6818|1=1623}} ({{ill|lt=Genna (Japan)|Q635245|WD=Q635245|lt=Genna|1=Genna}} 9)). Books and artifacts collected from various regions by Matsuoka Shirabe for research are preserved within the shrine as the “{{ill|lt=Tawa Bunko|Q11430642|WD=Q11430642|1=Tawa Bunko}} (Kōbokusha)” (Important Cultural Property). ; Principal deity * {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhime|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayakitsuhime-no-Mikoto}} ; Associated deities * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119|lt=Ōsasagi-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Nintoku}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Chūai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|lt=Tarashinakatsuhiko-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Chūai}} * {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|WD=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}} * {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|lt=Okinaga-Tarashihime-no-Mikoto|1=Empress Jingū}} * {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}} === Tawa Shrine (Maeyama, Sanuki City) === {{Shrine |Name= Tawa Shrine |Image= {{Image requested|cat=Sanuki City}} |Location= {{ill|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|Q161454|WD=Q161454|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Sanuki City|Q857202|WD=Q857202|1=Sanuki, Kagawa}}{{ill|lt=Maeyama|Q10898873|WD=Q10898873|1=Maeyama}} Aza Michi 2541 |Coordinates= {{coord|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E|display=inline}} |Enshrined deity= {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} disputed shrine |Founded= Unknown |Main hall= |Alternate name= Ōtao Myōjin |Annual festival= September 9 |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Kagawa|label=Tawa Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|12|36.4|N|134|11|6.7|E}}}} }} The date of foundation is unknown. A shrine record states: “From Yamato Province, the Ōmiwa deity was invited during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Junna|Q329713|WD=Q329713|1=Emperor Junnin}}, in the sixth month of the first year of Tenchō (842), to Tawa-go, Kankawa District, Sanuki Province, by the founder of the Shingon sect {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227|1=Kūkai}} together with Fujiwara Sa-daifu Masatomi, and thus was named Tawa Daimyōjin.” The {{ill|lt=Ōmiwa deity|Q705542|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Ōkami|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} is equated with Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto, matching the enshrined deity “{{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}.” The Ikoma Chronicle states, “This shrine (in Maeyama) is the Tawa Shrine of Kankawa District, Sanuki Province, listed in the Engishiki.” The Zensan-shi records: “Tawa Shrine is located in Ōtawa, Maeyama Village. The Gokuraku-ji record says: In the eighth year of Engi (908), in the fourth month of summer, Tawa Shrine and Minono-hiko Shrine were established; this likely refers to their being listed among the government shrines. Now only foundation stones remain, and within there is a small shrine.”{{Cite web |title=Zensan-shi {{!}} Kansai University Digital Archive |url=https://www.iiif.ku-orcas.kansai-u.ac.jp/hakuen_bunko/002500426#?page=1 |website=www.iiif.ku-orcas.kansai-u.ac.jp |access-date=2024-12-19}} ; Principal deity * {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} ; Associated deities * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taokihooi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} === Controversy concerning the Shikinaisha === In the late Edo period, {{ill|lt=Matsuoka Shirabe|Q6537602|WD=Q6537602|1=Matsuoka Mitsugi}}, the chief priest of Shido Hachimangū (an artist of the “Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province,” later serving as priest of Kotohira-gū), was also known as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kokugaku scholar|1=UNKNOWN}} and contributed greatly to the Takamatsu Domain. He suddenly asserted that “Shido Hachimangū is the Tawa Shrine (Sanuki Sannomiya) listed in the Engishiki.” In his work *Sanuki Province Official Shrines Examination*{{Cite web |title=National Diet Library Digital Collection |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/815400 |website=dl.ndl.go.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} (Meiji 12), Matsuoka wrote: “The Tawa Shrine in Maeyama enshrines [https://nihonsinwa.com/page/2634.html Koyosotahobime], {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Taokihooi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, or else {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, and is called Ōtao after their names, and therefore is not Tawa Shrine {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=※1|1=UNKNOWN}}. Just as there is a Kōzaki Shrine in Kōzaki-go, Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go. In a document called the ‘Onharai-fuchō’ {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=※2|1=UNKNOWN}}, which my Matsuoka family has inherited for generations from the Okada Daifu family, priests of the Outer Shrine, there is an entry for Shido-ura that reads ‘Tawa no kannushi-dono,’ which can be said to be proof that the shrine I serve is Tawa Shrine. Though worm-eaten, I reproduce a copy below. As shown by the Onharai-fuchō, Tama-no-ura (Shido Bay) is Tawa-no-ura, and the land of Shido is Tawa-go.” From the Meiji period onward, Shido Hachimangū thus came to be widely known as the Engishiki-listed Tawa Shrine. ※1 {{ill|lt=However, upon checking the Kagawa Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer, neither Koyosotahobime nor Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto are enshrined at the Maeyama Tawa Shrine.|UNKNOWN|lt=According to the Kagawa Prefecture Shrine Gazetteer, neither Koyosotahobime nor Ōtatane-ko-no-Mikoto are enshrined at the Maeyama Tawa Shrine.}} There are differing theories regarding the place name Ōtawa in Maeyama Village: one holds that “tawa” derives from “tawamu” (to bend), meaning a bent mountain, that is, a mountain pass; another suggests it comes from “tao” (tail of a rice field), meaning the end of the paddies extending from the plain (at the mountain’s edge).{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} ※2 Okada Daifu copy: “Sanuki Dōja Ippen Nikki,” Ansei 3 (1856), a record of visits made in Eiroku 8 (1565) by Ise shrine priests (Ise Onshi), messengers of Ise Grand Shrine, who toured their Sanuki parishioners to receive first-fruits offerings. It records their travel routes, regions, parishioner names, and the contents and quantities of Ise souvenirs distributed (held by Kanzakura Shrine, Kōnan Town).{{Cite web |title=Exhibited materials: “A City Open to the Sea—Takamatsu: 900 Years as a Port City—Special Exhibition” {{!}} NDL Search {{!}} National Diet Library |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I000009429918 |website=National Diet Library Search (NDL Search) |access-date=2025-01-22 |language=ja}} Meanwhile, as local historical research progressed, a theory emerged that Tawa-go was not Shido or Maeyama but Tsuruba in Tsuda Town. According to the *Newly Compiled History of Kagawa Prefecture*{{Cite web |title=Newly Compiled History of Kagawa Prefecture {{!}} NDL Search {{!}} National Diet Library |url=https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000002-I000000941988 |website=National Diet Library Search (NDL Search) |access-date=2024-12-20 |language=ja}} (published 1953), a “Daijōkan decree” held by {{ill|lt=Anrakuju-in|Q2852420|WD=Q2852420|lt=Anrakujū-ji|1=Anrakuju-in}} in Kyoto (dated August 19, {{ill|lt=Kōji|Q1194459|WD=Q1194459|1=Kōji (Heian period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1143|Q19617|WD=Q19617|lt=1143|1=1143}})) describes the boundaries of Tomita Estate, stating: “To the east, the boundary is the border of Ōuchi District; to the west, from within Ishida-go at the northeastern corner, the Funaki River (present-day Sendan River) and the southern great road of Ishizaki (Nankaidō), along the bank of Minami-izumi; to the south, the border with Awa Province; to the north, the mountain peaks forming the boundary between Tawa and Kanzaki.” Thus it is clear that to the north of Tomita Estate lay the two districts of Kanzaki and Tawa. This also accords with the old maps and travel routes (regions) of the Ise priests found in the copy of the “Onharai-fuchō” (= “Sanuki Dōja Ippen Nikki”) cited by Matsuoka Shirabe in *Sanuki Province Official Shrines Examination*. Therefore, if one accepts Matsuoka Shirabe’s premise that “Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go,” then the Tawa Shrine in Shido would also be in error. If one follows Matsuoka Shirabe’s assertion that “the shrine located in Tawa-go is Tawa Shrine,” there is also a theory that Ōmori Shrine, enshrined within the grounds of a Tenmangū located in the mountains of the Tsuruba area, should be identified as Tawa Shrine. However, due to a lack of sources, the whereabouts of the Sanuki Sannomiya “Tawa Shrine” remain unknown. There is no doubt that, throughout history, both the Tawa Shrine in Shido and the Tawa Shrine in Maeyama have been regarded and worshipped as the Engishiki-listed Tawa Shrine.{{Cite web |title=History of Shido Town (New Edition), Vols. 1–2 – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2037 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}}{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} If the premise that “Tawa Shrine must be in Tawa-go” collapses, or if definitive sources are discovered, history may yet be rewritten.{{Cite web |title=History of Nagao Town (Revised) – Kagawa Prefectural Library |url=https://www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp/know/local/local_2038 |website=www.library.pref.kagawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-12-20}} === References === * *Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province*{{Cite web |title=Illustrated Famous Places of Sanuki Province |url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/das/meta/F1000000000000002307.html |website=National Archives of Japan Digital Archive |access-date=2024-12-20 |language=ja |first=Independent Administrative Institution National Archives of |last=Japan}} (written by Kajiwara Kagesada, illustrated by Matsuoka Nobumasa [later Matsuoka Shirabe]), *Zensan-shi*, *Tamamo-shū*, *Origins of the Inner Palace of the Domain* === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} {{Shinto horizontal}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Tawa Shrine (Sanuki)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Tawa Shrine (Sanuki)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Tawa Shrine (Sanuki)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Sanuki
